sections.heading: Proposed Methods
paragraphs: We propose a multi-feature eye-tracking protocol designed to screen for ADHD in children aged 6–12 years in clinical settings. The protocol is built upon prior feasibility work demonstrating that a 15-minute eye-tracking assessment achieves 93% completion rates and high test-retest reliability in ADHD populations (Rodriguez et al., 2022).

Participants will complete a structured reading task consisting of three blocks: (1) a passive viewing block with natural scene images (baseline), (2) a word-reading block with single-word presentations, and (3) a passage-reading block with connected text. Eye movements will be recorded using a Tobii Pro Spectrum at 120 Hz. The primary outcome measures are: fixation-duration variability (standard deviation of fixation durations), mean saccadic latency, and blink rate per minute.

Feature extraction will be performed using the EyeLink Data Viewer and custom Python scripts. For each participant, we will extract the three primary features and train a random forest classifier with 10-fold cross-validation to discriminate ADHD from control participants. The model will be evaluated on sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve.

A target sample size of 120 participants (60 ADHD, 60 controls) has been calculated based on a power analysis (α = 0.05, β = 0.80, anticipated AUC = 0.78). The protocol is designed to be completed within 20 minutes total, including calibration, task administration, and debriefing, making it feasible for integration into routine clinical workflows.

arguments.premise: A 15-minute eye-tracking protocol has demonstrated 93% completion rates and high test-retest reliability in ADHD populations.
arguments.claim: A similar multi-feature protocol can be feasibly implemented in clinical settings for ADHD screening.

arguments.premise: Combining multiple eye-tracking features (fixation variability, saccadic latency, blink rate) captures complementary aspects of attentional dysfunction.
arguments.claim: A multi-feature classifier will achieve ≥80% sensitivity and ≥75% specificity.

references.formatted: Rodriguez, C., Gonzalez-Castro, P., & Cueli, M. (2022). Eye-tracking based attention assessment in children with ADHD: A feasibility study. Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology.
